questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: A radio station broadcasts at a frequency of \(98.1 \times 10^6\) Hz. What is the wavelength of this radiation in meters?
questions.explanation: The speed of light equation \(c = \lambda \nu\) rearranges to \(\lambda = c/\nu = (3.00 \times 10^8) / (98.1 \times 10^6) = 3.06\) m. Option A results from a division error by \(10^6\) instead of \(10^8\).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS

explanations_tiered.definition: The equation \(c = \lambda \nu\) relates the speed of light to wavelength and frequency.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Electromagnetic radiation travels at \(c = 3.00 \times 10^8\) m/s. Given frequency, wavelength is \(\lambda = c/\nu\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of waves on a rope: faster waves or higher frequency means shorter distance between peaks.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The constancy of \(c\) in vacuum means wavelength and frequency are inversely proportional: \(\lambda \propto 1/\nu\). The product always equals \(c\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that all EM radiation travels at \(c = 3.00 \times 10^8\) m/s.
Use \(c = \lambda \nu\).
Divide \(c\) by the given frequency to find wavelength.
misconceptions: Some confuse wavelength and frequency, thinking higher frequency means longer wavelength — actually inverse.
Forgetting to use Hz = s⁻¹ leads to unit errors.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: c = λν. λ = c/ν. Use consistent units.

glossary_entry.definition: Distance between successive peaks of a wave, related to frequency by \(c = \lambda \nu\).

flashcard.front: What is the wavelength of EM radiation with frequency 98.1 MHz?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate wavelength from frequency?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Distance over which a wave repeats, measured in meters.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to convert MHz to Hz correctly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate wavelength and frequency of EMR

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: What is the energy of a photon with a frequency of \(5.00 \times 10^{14}\) Hz?
questions.explanation: Using \(E = h\nu = (6.63 \times 10^{-34})(5.00 \times 10^{14}) = 3.32 \times 10^{-19}\) J. Option B is \(h\) alone; option C omits \(10^{-34}\) factor.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS

explanations_tiered.definition: Planck's equation \(E = h\nu\) gives the energy of a photon, where \(h\) is Planck's constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Energy is quantized; each photon carries energy proportional to its frequency. Higher frequency → higher energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each 'package' of light (photon) has energy. Higher frequency waves carry more energy per photon.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(E = h\nu\) with \(h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\) J·s and \(\nu = 5.00 \times 10^{14}\) Hz gives \(E = 3.32 \times 10^{-19}\) J.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Planck's quantization explained blackbody radiation. For a photon, \(E = h\nu = hc/\lambda\). This energy is delivered in discrete packets.
reasoning_steps: Recall that photon energy depends on frequency.
Use \(E = h\nu\).
Multiply Planck's constant by the given frequency.
misconceptions: Some think h is the energy, but it's a constant; energy requires multiplying by frequency.
Higher amplitude does not mean higher photon energy — photon energy depends only on frequency.

glossary_entry.definition: A particle of light carrying energy \(E = h\nu\), with no mass.

flashcard.front: Calculate the energy of a 5.00 × 10¹⁴ Hz photon.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the photon energy formula?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Discrete packet of electromagnetic energy, E = hν.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that h is tiny, so photon energies are very small.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate photon energy using Planck's equation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: What happens if light with frequency below the threshold frequency strikes a metal surface in the photoelectric effect?

options.B: Electrons are ejected with low kinetic energy
options.C: Electrons are ejected only if intensity is high

questions.hint: Each photon must have enough energy to overcome the work function.
questions.explanation: The photoelectric effect requires photon energy \(h\nu\) to be at least the work function \(h\nu_0\). Below threshold, no electrons are ejected regardless of intensity. Option C is incorrect — intensity only affects current once above threshold.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.content_anchor: A photon with energy less than E₀ (ν<ν₀) cannot remove an electron

explanations_tiered.definition: The threshold frequency is the minimum light frequency needed to eject electrons in the photoelectric effect.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Below threshold, each photon lacks the minimum energy (work function) to liberate an electron. Increasing intensity adds more photons but each still has insufficient energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine trying to knock a ball out of a hole with pebbles. If each pebble is too light, throwing more pebbles won't work.
explanations_tiered.intermediate:
Einstein explained that each photon's energy \(h
u\) must exceed the work function \(\phi = h
u_0\). Below \(
u_0\), no photon can eject an electron.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The photoelectric effect disproved classical wave theory (which predicted any frequency works with enough intensity). Quantum explanation: energy quantized, one photon transfers all energy to one electron. If \(h\nu < \phi\), no ejection.
reasoning_steps: Recall threshold frequency is minimum for electron ejection.
Below threshold, photon energy < work function.
No electrons ejected regardless of intensity.
misconceptions: Classical wave thinking suggests high intensity should eject electrons at any frequency — quantum theory disproves this.
Threshold frequency is material-dependent; not universal.

glossary_entry.definition: Minimum light frequency required to eject an electron from a metal surface.

flashcard.front: What happens if light frequency is below threshold in photoelectric effect?
flashcard.back: No electrons are ejected, regardless of intensity.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does low-frequency light fail to cause photoelectric effect?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Each photon's energy is below the work function.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Minimum frequency needed to eject an electron.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that increasing intensity cannot substitute for sufficient frequency.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Below threshold frequency, electrons are not ejected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: What does a line spectrum (atomic emission spectrum) of an element represent?

options.A: Specific wavelengths of light emitted by excited electrons
options.B: All wavelengths of light absorbed by the element
options.C: Continuous range of colors from white light
options.D: Wavelengths emitted by the nucleus only

questions.hint: Electrons jumping down between fixed energy levels emit fixed wavelengths.
questions.explanation: A line spectrum consists of discrete wavelengths emitted when electrons drop from higher to lower energy levels. Option B describes absorption spectra; option C is a continuous spectrum (e.g., sunlight).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.content_anchor: only a few colors are observed, displayed as a series of individual lines

explanations_tiered.definition: An atomic emission spectrum is a set of discrete wavelengths emitted by excited atoms as electrons return to lower energy levels.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each element has a unique line spectrum because its electron energy levels are quantized and unique.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a ladder: you can only step between specific rungs. Light comes only at certain colors, not all colors.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: When electrons drop from higher n to lower n, they emit photons with energies \(\Delta E = h\nu\), producing sharp lines at specific wavelengths.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Rydberg formula predicts hydrogen's line wavelengths: \(1/\lambda = R_H(1/n_f^2 - 1/n_i^2)\). Each series (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen) corresponds to different final n.
reasoning_steps: Recall that electrons occupy discrete energy levels.
Transitions between levels emit photons of specific energies.
Specific energies → specific wavelengths → line spectrum.
misconceptions: Line spectrum is not continuous — it has gaps because only certain transitions allowed.
Absorption spectrum (dark lines) is different from emission spectrum (bright lines).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Line spectrum: discrete wavelengths from electron transitions.

glossary_entry.definition: Set of discrete wavelengths emitted by excited atoms returning to lower energy states.

flashcard.front: What does a line spectrum represent?
flashcard.back: Specific wavelengths emitted when electrons fall between quantized levels.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is an atomic emission spectrum?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Discrete wavelengths from excited electrons falling to lower energy levels.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Unique set of colored lines emitted by an excited element.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show hydrogen Balmer series as classic example.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Line spectrum corresponds to specific wavelengths emitted by electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: Which series of hydrogen emission lines lies primarily in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum?
questions.hint: The visible lines from hydrogen are named after their discoverer.
questions.explanation: Balmer series transitions (n ≥ 3 → n = 2) emit visible light. Lyman (UV), Paschen (IR), Brackett (IR) are outside visible range.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.content_anchor: Balmer series was particularly easy to study because some its lines fall in the visible range

explanations_tiered.definition: The Balmer series includes hydrogen emission lines with final energy level n = 2, several of which are in the visible region.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Balmer lines (Hα, Hβ, Hγ, Hδ) are visible. Lyman ends at n=1 (UV); Paschen ends at n=3 (IR).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Balmer lines are the colorful lines you see when hydrogen gas glows in a tube.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Transitions from higher n to n=2 emit visible light. Red Hα (656 nm) is n=3→2; blue Hβ is n=4→2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Rydberg formula: \(1/\lambda = R_H(1/2^2 - 1/n_i^2)\) for Balmer series. Visible wavelengths (400–700 nm) correspond to n_i = 3,4,5,6.
reasoning_steps: Recall visible light range ~400–700 nm.
Balmer series (n → 2) produces visible lines.
Lyman (→1) UV, Paschen (→3) IR.
misconceptions: Lyman series is often confused as visible — it's ultraviolet.
Not all hydrogen series are visible; Balmer is the only visible one for hydrogen.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Balmer: visible. Lyman: UV. Paschen: IR.

glossary_entry.definition: Hydrogen emission lines from transitions ending at n = 2, many in visible region.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Name the hydrogen series in the visible range.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Set of hydrogen emission lines in the visible region.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Mention that 656 nm red line is most famous.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the line spectrum of hydrogen
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Balmer series falls in visible region.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: What is the energy of a photon with wavelength \(500 \text{ nm}\)?
questions.explanation: Convert 500 nm to \(5.00 \times 10^{-7}\) m. \(E = hc/\lambda = (6.63 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)/(5.00 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.98 \times 10^{-19}\) J. Option A omits c; option C has incorrect exponent.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS

explanations_tiered.definition: Photon energy is inversely proportional to wavelength: \(E = hc/\lambda\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Shorter wavelength means higher energy. Convert nm to m before calculating.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Blue light (shorter λ) has more energy per photon than red light (longer λ).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(E = hc/\lambda\) with λ = 500 × 10⁻⁹ m gives \(E = 3.98 × 10⁻¹⁹\) J.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This equation combines Planck's constant, speed of light, and wavelength. It shows energy quantized as \(h\nu\), but since \(\nu = c/\lambda\), \(E = hc/\lambda\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Convert 500 nm to meters: 500 × 10⁻⁹ m = 5.00 × 10⁻⁷ m.
reasoning_steps: Use E = hc/λ.
Convert wavelength to meters.
Plug constants and compute.

formula_analysis.name: Photon energy from wavelength
formula_analysis.derivation: E = hν, ν = c/λ → E = hc/λ

misconceptions: Forgetting to convert nm to m leads to answer off by factor 10⁹.
Using λ in nm without conversion yields very small wrong E.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: E = hc/λ. Convert nm to m.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy of a photon equals \(hc/\lambda\), where λ is wavelength.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to find photon energy from wavelength?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: E = hc/λ; convert λ to meters first.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: E = hc/λ, relating energy to wavelength.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to always convert to meters.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.subsection: 1.5.6 The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
questions.structural_anchor: The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
questions.question: Which statement best describes the wave-particle duality of light?

options.A: Light exhibits both wave properties and particle properties
options.B: Light is only a wave, not a particle
options.C: Light is only a particle, not a wave
options.D: Light is neither a wave nor a particle

questions.hint: Light can show interference (wave) and photoelectric effect (particle).
questions.explanation: Wave-particle duality means light has both wave-like (diffraction, interference) and particle-like (photons, photoelectric effect) properties. Options B and C are incomplete; D is false.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.6 The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
source_trace.structural_element: The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
source_trace.content_anchor: EMR ... shows certain characteristics of particulate matter as well ... dual nature of light

explanations_tiered.definition: Wave-particle duality is the concept that light and matter exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Experiments like diffraction show wave nature; photoelectric effect shows particle nature. Both are correct.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Light acts like a wave (ripples in a pond) and like a particle (tiny bullets) depending on how you measure it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Interference and diffraction require wave model; photoelectric effect and Compton scattering require photon (particle) model.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Quantum mechanics describes objects with wave functions. The de Broglie relation \(\lambda = h/p\) links particle momentum to wavelength.
reasoning_steps: Recall that light shows wave behavior (e.g., interference).
Also shows particle behavior (photons, photoelectric effect).
Both are true → wave-particle duality.
misconceptions: Some think scientists discarded wave model after Einstein — they didn't; both models are needed.
Duality does not mean light switches; it means both descriptions are valid.

glossary_entry.definition: The principle that light and matter exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

flashcard.back: Light and matter have both wave and particle properties.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does light behave as a wave or particle?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Light has both wave and particle characteristics.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Light is both wave and particle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that experiments dictate which model is useful.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the dual nature of light
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Light shows both wave and particle properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.subsection: 1.5.6 The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
questions.structural_anchor: The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
questions.question: What is the de Broglie wavelength of an electron moving at \(1.00 \times 10^6\) m/s?
questions.explanation: De Broglie wavelength \(\lambda = h/(mv) = (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) / [(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.00 \times 10^6)] = 7.28 \times 10^{-10}\) m. Option B results from a factor of 10 error in velocity.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.6 The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy
source_trace.structural_element: The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy

explanations_tiered.definition: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is \(\lambda = h/(mv)\), relating momentum to wavelength.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Matter has wave properties; faster or heavier particles have shorter wavelengths.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even tiny particles like electrons have a wavelength, but it's extremely small.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(\lambda = h/(mv)\) with electron mass and given velocity yields \(7.28 \times 10^{-10}\) m, about the size of an atom.
explanations_tiered.advanced: De Broglie's relation \(\lambda = h/p\) is central to quantum mechanics. Electron diffraction experiments confirmed matter waves.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write de Broglie equation: \(\lambda = h/(mv)\).
reasoning_steps: Recall \(\lambda = h/(mv)\).
Use electron mass and given velocity.
Compute wavelength.

formula_analysis.name: de Broglie wavelength
formula_analysis.derivation: From E = mc² = hν and λ = h/p

misconceptions: Some think only light has wavelength; de Broglie showed matter does too.
Macroscopic objects have extremely small λ → wave effects unnoticeable.

glossary_entry.definition: The wavelength associated with a moving particle, given by \(\lambda = h/(mv)\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate de Broglie wavelength of an electron at 1.00×10⁶ m/s.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Wavelength of a particle: λ = h/(mv).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that λ decreases as velocity increases.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate de Broglie wavelength of particles

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.subsection: 1.5.4 The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.structural_anchor: The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: According to Bohr's model, why do hydrogen atoms emit only specific wavelengths of light?

options.A: Electrons can only exist in certain allowed energy levels
options.C: Light is emitted only when electrons are stationary

questions.hint: Quantized orbits produce quantized photon energies.
questions.explanation: Bohr postulated that electrons occupy discrete energy levels. Transitions between levels emit photons with energy \(\Delta E = h\nu\), producing specific λ. Option B is false; D is true but not the reason for line spectrum.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.4 The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
source_trace.structural_element: The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
source_trace.content_anchor: energy changes in an atom are quantized; only certain orbits allowed

explanations_tiered.definition: Bohr's model quantizes electron energy levels, so only certain photon energies (hence wavelengths) are emitted.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Electrons jump between fixed energy levels. The energy difference determines photon wavelength via \(\Delta E = hc/\lambda\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like coins in a vending machine: you can only drop in certain coin values (energy), so you get only certain snacks (light colors).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Allowed orbits have energies \(E_n = -R_H/n^2\). Transition from n=3 to n=2 gives a specific ΔE, producing one specific λ.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Bohr's postulates: \(mvr = n\hbar\), \(E_n = -13.6/n^2\) eV. The Rydberg formula \(1/\lambda = R_H(1/n_f^2 - 1/n_i^2)\) exactly matches observed lines.
reasoning_steps: Recall Bohr's idea: electrons occupy discrete orbits.
Each orbit has fixed energy.
Jumping between orbits releases a fixed ΔE → fixed λ.
misconceptions: Electrons do not 'spiral' into nucleus in Bohr model; they are stable in allowed orbits.
Bohr model only works for hydrogen-like atoms, not multi-electron atoms.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Bohr: discrete energy levels → discrete λ.

glossary_entry.definition: Model of hydrogen atom with quantized electron orbits and energy levels.

flashcard.front: Why does Bohr model predict line spectrum?
flashcard.back: Electrons in quantized levels emit photons of specific ΔE.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does hydrogen emit only specific colors?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Quantized energy levels produce specific ΔE values.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Model with quantized electron orbits explaining line spectra.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain line spectrum of hydrogen using Bohr model
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Quantized energy levels produce specific wavelengths.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.subsection: 1.5.4 The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.structural_anchor: The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: When an electron in a hydrogen atom moves from \(n = 3\) to \(n = 1\), what happens?
questions.hint: Transition to lower energy level releases energy.
questions.explanation: Electron dropping to lower n emits a photon. Absorption happens when going to higher n. Ionization would require n → ∞.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.4 The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
source_trace.structural_element: The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
source_trace.content_anchor: emitting light when it falls to a lower energy orbit

explanations_tiered.definition: An electron moving from a higher to a lower energy level emits a photon of energy equal to the level difference.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Emission occurs when \(n_i > n_f\). The photon's wavelength corresponds to \(\Delta E = hc/\lambda\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like rolling down a hill: electron loses energy and gives off light.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Transition 3 → 1 has \(\Delta E = -2.18\times10^{-18}(1/1^2 - 1/3^2) = -1.94\times10^{-18}\) J, emitted as UV photon.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The energy difference is \(E_3 - E_1 = 13.6(1/1^2 - 1/3^2) = 12.09\) eV, corresponding to Lyman series (UV).
reasoning_steps: Compare initial n=3 and final n=1.
Final is lower energy.
Excess energy released as photon.
misconceptions: Some think absorption and emission both produce photons — absorption removes photons, emission creates them.
Ionization is only when n → ∞, not just n=3→1.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: n high → n low: emits photon.

glossary_entry.definition: Electron drop from higher to lower energy level, emitting a photon.

flashcard.front: n=3 to n=1 in hydrogen: absorb or emit?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does n=3→1 in hydrogen absorb or emit light?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Emits a photon (electron falls to lower energy).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Release of photon when electron moves to lower energy level.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain that atoms emit energy when undergoing transitions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Moving to lower energy emits a photon.
